english: which (out of three or more choices)

romaji: Moshi moshi (some people pronounce this mushi mushi)

hiragana: よろしくおねがいします。/ おねがいします
げんきです。/ まあまあです
どうもありがとうございます
どういたしまして。/ いいえいいえ

notes: Since we don't know any verbs yet, having example sentences isn't the best way to practice these words. What I want you to do is: go about your day, and if you see someone, try to think of the appropriate pronoun for them, and also think of the suffix that you would attach to their name. This will be great practice!

Since we don't know any verbs yet, having example sentences isn't the best way to practice these words. What I want you to do is: go about your day, and if you see someone, try to think of the appropriate pronoun for them, and also think of the suffix that you would attach to their name. This will be great practice!
As you can see, these words are taken from English. OL stands for Office Lady.
As you can see, these words are taken from English. OL stands for Office Lady.
In Japanese, the leg and foot are one unit.
In Japanese, for toes, they literally say foot finger.
In Japanese, for toes, they literally say foot finger.
Kome refers to the rice itself. Gohan refers to a bowl of rice.

Japanese people traditionally eat rice with every meal, therefore morning rice bowl became the word for breakfast. The second word, choushoku means early meal, and is used more often to refer to anything that is eaten in the morning. The same rule applies to the next two words.
Japan is crowded, so instead of having central police stations, they have small scattered police stations called boxes throughout the cities.
Machi can mean town or city. Shi is the suffix used for cities. For example, Yokohama City is pronounced Yokohama-shi.
Machi can mean town or city. Shi is the suffix used for cities. For example, Yokohama City is pronounced Yokohama-shi.
This can also refer to bowls, any type of dish.
This is a small bowl used for rice or tea. The word itself actually means tea bowl.
This word is also used with time.
Don't confuse this word with grandfather.
Don't confuse this word with grandfather.
Don't confuse this word with grandmother.
Don't confuse this word with grandmother.

The kanji for this word depends on the gender of the cousin. It can be written six different ways, but is always pronounced the same. The word is usually just written without kanji, however. The order in the brackets above is as follows: male cousins, older male cousin, younger male cousin, female cousins, older female cousin, younger female cousin.

Similar to cousin, the kanji for this word can be written four different ways, however, the kanji for two brothers is most often used, even when referring to women. The order above is male siblings, mixed siblings with younger sister, female siblings, mixed siblings with younger brother.
Cell phone is a rare word that uses sometimes uses katakana. The kanji doesn't refer to a cell phone specifically, but rather, anything carried in your hand.
Cell phone is a rare word that uses sometimes uses katakana. The kanji doesn't refer to a cell phone specifically, but rather, anything carried in your hand.
This often refers to luggage but also just things you are carrying or taking with you somewhere.
This refers to an electric train, opposed to a steam engine. Most all public trains in Japan are electric.
This refers to temples for the Buddhist religion.
Shinto is a religion that is native to Japan, that was popular before Buddhism. Though most Japanese people are non religious, it is still customary to go to shrines and temples on certain occasions.
If you have to compare this to something in western culture, it's similar to a bed and breakfast.
Wards are used for large cities, while districts are used for smaller cities.
Wards are used for large cities, while districts are used for smaller cities.
If you notice, this kanji is two trees.
This kanji is three trees, an even bigger amount!
The first kanji is for river, the second for stream.
This refers to the entrance itself as well as the area around the entrance.
This refers to a Japanese style sliding door.

The first kanji is the general word for see, while the second is used to write watch as part of the audience, for instance, in a theater or sports arena. As you continue your studies, you will find many words like this, that have different kanji for different meanings of the same word.
This verb almost always refers to returning home.
This verb implies working hard. You can not use it to say you work for a company.

english: to say / to tell / to be called
to sleep / to go to bed / to lie down

notes: This verb also means to rouse.

english: to wake up / to open your eyes
to turn off / to extinguish / to erase

notes: The second kanji is used for taking pictures.
Investigate is a bit formal in English. This word is more casual in Japanese, and can mean to search for information or find out. This word is also used to say look up words.

english: to enjoy oneself / to have fun

notes: This word is also used for seat belts, as in fasten.
Be careful with this word. It is used to say that two things are different, as in not the same, and also used to say that something is wrong, as in not correct.

english: to stick / to glue / to attach

notes: This is used for the weather, referring to rain or snow falling.

english: to go for a walk (do walk)
to take out / to get out / to put out
to take out / to pull out

notes: The first kanji is for packed, the second for crowded. This first kanji is used for many compound verbs, and it has a sense of inclusion. The second kanji meaning is crowded or mixture.
The first kanji means association or unite. So this word has the sense of putting things together.
A secondary meaning of this word is to contain. When people ask what ingredients were used to make a food, they will literally ask What has entered this dish?
A secondary meaning of this word is to tell. As in, when you are telling someone some information, you are teaching them something they didn't know before.
This is limited to instruments that don't involve blowing air.
This is used for playing wind instruments, as well as to say that things are blowing, such as the wind.

This word is a bit complicated. The core meaning of this word is more along the lines of to put out. We get the meaning shine from the idea that the sun is putting out light. This word can mean to wear with swords (more important back in the day than now). It can mean to serve / to offer, as in a cup of tea. It can mean to put your arms out, and also it is used for putting up an umbrella.
The emphasis on this word is information going into your head, as well as being in the state of being able to recall the information.
The emphasis on this word is the information coming out of your head. This compound verb literally means exiting memory.
In English there is the comical distinction between loving someone and being in love with someone. This word means in love.
To say that you are married, you will use the Te-Iru form of this verb. If you use the simple present tense, you are asking someone to marry you, so watch out!

english: to be similar / to look the same

notes: Instead of take, in Japanese we say enter a bath.
This is used to say an exact amount, as in money or time. In English we can sometimes respond to something, showing our agreement by saying, Exactly! But this word can not be used in that context.
This word is often used with commands to tell someone to do something properly, as in, give a real effort.
This is often used to tell someone to make certain that they do something.

english: not necessarily / not always the case

notes: This word is often used idiomatically by itself to say: I'm not entirely certain what your saying is true, or, What you just said has raised more questions than answers. What the speaker is saying, is that the situation has become delicate, because it would be impolite to ask certain questions to get the truthful answers.

Instead of saying the name of the electric device, many Japanese people will just talk about turning on the electricity of the device. So instead of saying, Turn on the lights, they will say Turn on the electricity.

english: to receive / to get / to take a test
notes: This word's core meaning is to receive, but has a lot of uses in this context. It is also used in many compound verbs, as we will soon see. It is not usually used for obtaining physical objects, but rather, in sentences like, The speech was well received. Giving and receiving is actually a bit more complicated and is covered in detail in Volume Two.

english: to fold / to snap / to break
notes: The kanji here means to fold, and this word is often used to say that something broke in half.

english: to break / to divide / to crack
notes: This is the same word as mathematical division. It can also mean to split, to cut, to slice. The kanji for this word has the symbol for a sword on the right, so that may help you remember the meaning.
This is the word you will use for machines or devices when they stop working.
In Western countries, sake refers to only rice wine, but in Japan, it refers to any type of alcohol.
Again, compare the kanji in this word to the others for pronunciation.
Again, compare the kanji in this word to the others for pronunciation.
The noun nyuugaku means matriculation or enrollment.
These words are homonyms but have different kanji.
This word is used for the height of humans, not buildings or mountains.
This word can be used for any length, not just humans.
The second kanji isn't a kanji, it's a circle. It's there because in written Japanese, they often use circles to express the word correct.
Again, not a kanji, but a mark used to say incorrect.
Literally this means three angles.
Interpreters are different from translators in that their job is to convey the core meaning of the original speakers intent, rather than a word for word translation.
This usually refers to things being sold, a store's goods.
This doesn't mean to go outside, but rather to go off, as in the lights. This verb is intransitive.

I bet you don’t know the difference between a book shelf and a book case! Don’t worry, no one knows in Japanese either. If we deconstruct the kanji, bookshelf literally means book ledge and book case literally means book box.

I bet you don’t know the difference between a book shelf and a book case! Don’t worry, no one knows in Japanese either. If we deconstruct the kanji, bookshelf literally means book ledge and book case literally means book box.

english: to attach / to join / to add
notes: This verb can be used in many contexts where you are attaching or joining things together. It is used more often in compound verbs than by itself.
This word refers to the water, not the sand. In English we usually say Let’s go to the beach, but in Japanese they will say Let’s go to the sea.
The kanji is also used in cyberspace language to mean LOL.
The word opine is not often used in English, but this verb is used when you want to give your opinion.
Use this verb when you want to say think about.
This verb is a bit special, because to be born is the passive form of to give birth.
This verb is a bit special, because to be born is the passive form of to give birth.
Note that this verb can also be translated as to return.
Note that this verb can also be translated as to return.

english: to be the work of / to be the result of / to be done by

notes: This is an idiomatic phrase.
This usually just translates to talk, but has the implied meaning that you went up to someone and started talking to them.
The first kaji refers to fix and the second to heal. Note that these are the transitive form of the verbs.

english: to get used to / to grow accustomed to

notes: This is used for transferring residential locations as well as job locations, or job departments within a company. It can also mean to shift as is shifting topics, or spread / be contagious.
The first kanji is for general going down, the second is for getting off trains.
This verb is different in that it has a sense of not changing something completely, but merely making modifications or alterations to it.
This verb has a sense of a new version, replacing something or revising an old thing with something new. The core meaning of the kanji is to reform.

english: to bake / to grill / to roast / to burn

notes: On a cultural note, most Japanese people will buy snacks as souvenirs whenever they travel somewhere, for their friends, family, or co-workers. If you are going to Japan and plan on staying with someone, be sure to bring them a souvenir from your home country. They will really appreciate it!
This kanji as well as the previous ones are important to learn if you ever hope to operate the controls of a Japanese air conditioner.
This refers to your metaphorical heart, not your physical one.

english: to fail / to make a mistake

notes: This can be used as a slang to say fail a test. This word is also the verb for snowboarding.
This refers to raising children.
This is used for animals.
This word is used for all types of facial hair, with the three kanji representing the three English words respectively. These are probably not the most important kanji to memorize.
This is the body part, not the box of gold.
This is the body part.
This literally means finger circle.
This literally means hand bag.
This is also the school subject social studies.
This refers to the organization, not the individual officers.
This is not used to say the streets are congested, but rather it is the noun referring to the cars and trains.
The expensive definition comes from high price.
This refers to short in length or height.
This refers to short as in low to the ground.
The first kanji is the meaning easy, the second cheap. The cheap definition comes from easy to buy.

If you notice, big and small can sometimes be both I-Adjectives and Na-Adjectives. As a non-native speaker, you should always use them as I-Adjectives, however, in certain situations, Japanese speakers with use them as Na-Adjectives.

english: a small amount / a little bit
notes: Just like in English, this is actually not an adjective, but a noun. It can sometimes function like an adjective, but requires no special grammar.
In English, we usually don't say skillful, but rather good.
Again, for unskillful, we will just translate this as bad.
The first kanji means early, while the second means fast.
This word for hot as well as the next word for cold will only be used when you talk about the weather or how your whole body feels. For objects, we will learn different words later.
People also use this word to say shut up. Literally, they are saying: Loud!
This word is most commonly used to say someone has done something bad.
The three kanji are for the type of material that is hard. The first kanji meaning is to harden, the second is to be stiff, the third means strict.
The first kanji refers to the softness as opposed to hardness. The second refers to soft a soft material, like a blanket, or as in tender, like meat, or a flexible person.
Like a lot of Japanese adjectives dealing with difficulty, this word can also be used to say you are in a bad situation or not doing well.
On a cultural note, the kanji used in this word is used to count to five, because it has five strokes, similar to the four lines with a cross we use in English.
This word can also be the noun difference, when it is, it will be used like a No-Adjective. It is also often attached to words as a prefix and suffix.
This refers to the dilution of something, like a thin soup or a weak coffee.

This word doesn't actually mean wonderful. It is used in combinations with other adjectives to mean very. It is also used to said you have had enough or satisfied, as in food or drink. Or if you are in a store and a clerk asks if you need help, you can use this word to say you are fine and don't need help.
The kanji for this word together mean unusually big. It almost always is used in a negative context. When used by itself, it will mean you are in a difficult or bad situation.
We learned hot and cold before, but this word as well as the next word are used for objects.
In Day 31 we learned the word to fall. We use this verb with rain to say It's raining.
This word is used for physical locations, not abstractly.
This word is more often used abstractly, and is also used in a few grammar constructions, which we will learn in Volume Two.
Green is a relatively recent color in Japanese. Before it's introduction, Japanese people saw blue and green as the same color. This word can also refer to greenery, as in plants.
This word can also mean gloomy or depressing.
This is a noun that means location, but next to some object.
This refers to a horizontal position or movement. Like laying on your side, or shaking your head side to side.
This word can cause some confusion because it is often used negatively to mean something is strange or weird, so context is important.

english: Oh I see. / I understand now
notes: This is used to say that you now understand information that was previously unclear.
This literally means ten parts. I suppose ten parts is enough for one person?
Said by itself, this is used to express your sorrow, something like That's too bad or What a pity.
Said by itself, this word means long time no see.
This word is both the adjective and the noun.

This is probably the most popular word to use in Japanese to say things like great, awesome, wonderful. If you look it up, the definition says terrible, but it is usually used in a positive sense, just like terrific is a positive word in English that is derived from the negative word terror. It is also combined with other adjectives to mean very.
This refers to flavor, not a person's temperament.
This can be used with both positive and negative sentences, however, when it is used alone, it will mean not at all.

The kanji in this word are quite common. The first kanji means to calculate and is used in many compound words where calculations are done. The second kanji means number and is used in many compound words with numbers.
This is the noun of the previous verb.
This doesn't refer to the number set itself, but rather figures or summations on documents and things like that.
The first word is the concept of time, used in phrases like at that time, the second is an interval of time, used in phrases like we have time.
This means day of the Sun.
This means day of the Moon.
This means day of fire.
This means day of water.
This means day of trees.
This means day of gold.
This means day of earth.
These words can also be used to mean morning and afternoon.
These words can also be used to mean morning and afternoon.
This is used with a negative verb.
This means something will happen soon, any minute now.
In Japanese, when you say this word for soon, you mean very soon.
This word is more along the lines of the English meaning of soon.
This usually refers to the far future, with spaceships and robots.
This usually refers to the foreseeable future.

english: the past / ancient times / a long time ago
notes: This refers to things that happened long ago. But because time is relative, if it feels like a long time for the speaker, even a few days or weeks can feel like ancient times.
This refers to the more recent past.
Though these are two different words, these trains are faster than local trains, but slower than the express trains. JR will use semi-express while the Metro will use rapid.
Though these are two different words, these trains are faster than local trains, but slower than the express trains. JR will use semi-express while the Metro will use rapid.

This is the fastest of the trains and sometimes requires a premium. However, it is not often, and usually when it does, the train will look different than the other trains, so don't worry about getting on a train without paying enough.
This can be confusing because it has two completely opposite meanings. However, from the tense of the sentence you can understand which meaning it is.
This is the most basic word for recently
This word literally means during this interval.
This word literally means around this time.

english: this time / next time / another time
notes: This can sometimes be confusing because it can refer to an action taking place now, or be used to say to do something another time, so pay attention to the context.
This is another word that means recently. This word explicitly states the the thing has happened recently before. In English we can sometimes explicitly say this by saying just recently.

english: a little while / in a moment

notes: This is not used for comparisons. It is most often used in compound words, and to show limits and expectations.
This is also not used for comparisons. An idiomatic usage of this word, is to say it at the end of a speech, or when you are ordering food, to say That's all.
This is used for time intervals to say some event occurred within an allotted time frame.

english: to come / to go / to be
to come / to go / to be

notes: This word can be confusing because it also has some non-humble homonyms which use the same kanji. They include to be madly in love, to be defeated, to be annoyed.

english: to eat / to drink / to receive
notes: This is actually one of the first words we learned, that we use before we eat something, but as you can see, it has other contexts as well.

english: Never mind. / Don't worry about it
notes: This word comes from don't mind, but is used to tell someone to not worry about something, that it's not important.
This comes from the word mansion in English, but doesn't refer to a large house, but rather a building with multiple apartments or condominiums. These types of buildings are very popular in Japan.
This refers to people on TV, many of whom are famous for nothing other than being on TV.
This word does not refer to a woman's rights advocate, but quite the opposite. It refers to a man who pampers and takes care of women, often financially.
This doesn't refer to seals but rather stickers.
Not consent, but rather an electric outlet.
Japanese people will become very confused if you refer to your bicycle as a bike.

While trump is indeed a game you can play with cards, in Japanese it refers to the cards themselves. Conclusion Congratulations! You've finished the book. So, what to do now? Your goal as a Japanese speaker should be to pass the JLPT (Japanese Language Proficiency Test). There are five levels, N5 to N1, the latter being the most difficult. To work for a Japanese company, you generally need to pass the N2 test. To work as a translator, you generally need to pass the N1 test. Assuming you can use everything taught in this book with a fair amount of efficiency, you are now a high beginner level speaker of Japanese. As of now, you should be able to pass the N5 with ease, and though you haven't learned all of the grammar or vocabulary, you might have a shot at passing the N4. If you haven't already, you really should start studying kanji as well. You need to know about 2500 kanji to be able to read most things. If you practice about 5 kanji a day, this will take you around two years. Again, in my opinion, the best kanji learning resource available is the Kanji Learner's Course by Andrew Scott Conning (Kodansha USA, 2013), which can be used with an excellent series of graded reading sets and a sequential wall chart. If you decide to purchase Volume Two of this series, you will learn the rest of the grammar and vocabulary for the N4 test as well as the N3. Before you move on to Volume Two, please make sure you have mastered everything in this book. Take a bit more time to review everything once more before moving on. Whatever you decide to do, I wish you the best of luck! Study hard! Study every day! -KM
